---
title: "Phase B — Fee vs. Principal Simulator"
subtitle: "Interactive companion to the research note"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .metric-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .metric-grid .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.75rem; background: #fafafa; }
  .metric-grid .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .metric-grid .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.2rem; }
  .callout-note-lite { border-left: 3px solid #4c78a8; background: #f3f7fb; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; margin: 0.8rem 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }
  .legend-table { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.5rem 0 1rem; }
  .legend-table td:first-child { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; padding-right: 0.8rem; }
</style>
```

## What this report is about (for new readers)

Imagine a company that helps businesses retire tonnes of carbon-offset credits
on a blockchain protocol (here, Klima). To retire one tonne, the company must
first buy the protocol's token on the open market, and the token's dollar
price moves around every day. The company has to decide **how to charge
customers** and **how to source inventory**, and those two choices together
determine how much money it makes and how much risk it runs.

This notebook compares two business designs for that company:

- **Fee-based.** The company quotes the customer a markup on top of the live
  token price and buys just-in-time. It never holds a big inventory and it
  never takes a directional bet, but its revenue rises and falls with the
  token price.
- **Principal.** The company quotes the customer a **fixed dollar price per
  tonne today**, locking in the sale — and then takes on the job of finding
  the tokens. That can be done by (3a) buying all the tokens up front, (3b)
  buying them one-by-one as retirements happen, or (3c) any blend of the two.

The fee model behaves like a commission-taker. The principal model behaves
like a market-maker that has shorted a stream of forwards on the token: it
locked in a sale price and now has to cover it at an unknown cost. The key
question for the business is: *given how the token price is expected to move,
which model makes more money, which one loses more in a bad scenario, and how
much does pre-buying inventory (the "hedge ratio" $\alpha$) reduce that risk?*

The companion [research note](phase-a.qmd) derives the math. This
notebook runs the numbers: it simulates thousands of possible futures for the
token price, computes the company's profit and loss (P&L) in each one, and
plots the distributions.

::: {.callout-note-lite}
**Minimum background to read this page.** Familiarity with probability
(mean, standard deviation, quantiles) and with Monte Carlo simulation
helps, but the prose in each section explains what every plot is showing
and what a "good" or "bad" pattern looks like. Readers who want the
derivations should keep the [Phase A note](phase-a.qmd) open alongside; readers who just
want the business intuition can skim past the equations.
:::

## Overview

This notebook is the **Phase B** companion to the
[Phase A research note](phase-a.qmd) (the mathematical note). Phase A
wrote down closed-form formulas for the
mean and variance of every book's P&L under a standard price model
(Geometric Brownian Motion, or GBM — see the next section). Phase B, this
notebook, has three jobs:

1. **Cross-check the math.** For quantities the Phase A note gives in
   closed form, we also compute them by Monte Carlo simulation. If the
   two disagree, one of them is wrong. The `z` column in the §4 table is
   a numerical sanity check built exactly for this purpose.
2. **Fill in what closed form cannot give.** Tail-risk metrics (VaR,
   CVaR, loss probability — all defined below) depend on the full
   distribution, not just its first two moments. We estimate those
   empirically.
3. **Let the reader explore.** The $(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ grid at the bottom of the
   page is interactive: drag the sliders to see how the tail of the
   principal book responds to drift, volatility, and the hedge ratio.

All Monte Carlo computation happens in TypeScript (`src/`); this page only
reads the emitted JSON and visualises it. If you are running the report
locally, regenerate the data before previewing:

```sh
npm run simulate -- --seed 42
npm run sweep -- --seed 42
```

::: {.callout-note-lite}
**What you will see below.** Four kinds of plots: (1) a scorecard table of
closed-form vs Monte Carlo P&L moments with tail metrics, (2) overlaid P&L
distributions for the three stochastic models, (3) diagnostics on the shared
random kernel $I_T := \int_0^T S_t\,dt$ (the time-integral of the simulated price;
both the fee and back-to-back books turn out to be linear functions of this
single quantity, so it is worth looking at directly), and (4) parameter
sweeps showing how tail risk and the break-even quote $Q^*$ respond to drift
$\mu$, volatility $\sigma$, and hedge ratio $\alpha$.
:::

### Mini-glossary

If you are coming from outside quant finance, these three concepts come up
repeatedly on this page:

- **Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM).** A standard model for prices that
  evolve by random percentage moves (not random dollar moves). Two
  parameters: $\mu$ is the expected growth rate per year ("drift"), $\sigma$ is
  the size of the random jitter per year ("volatility"). This is the same
  model underneath Black–Scholes.
- **Monte Carlo simulation.** Rolling the dice many thousands of times.
  Here each "roll" is a full random trajectory of the token price over the
  horizon $T$, and we compute the company's P&L along that trajectory.
  Averaging over thousands of trajectories gives us an estimate of
  expected P&L; looking at the worst outcomes gives us tail risk.
- **Hedge ratio $\alpha$.** The fraction of the tokens the company pre-buys at
  day zero instead of buying just-in-time. $\alpha = 0$ means no pre-buying at
  all (maximum exposure to price); $\alpha = 1$ means buying the whole year's
  supply at inception (fully pre-hedged, zero terminal risk); $\alpha = 0.5$
  means half now, half later.

### Notation cheat-sheet

A brief reminder of the symbols used throughout (full derivations in the
[Phase A note](phase-a.qmd) §1–§5). Most symbols have a short English name in
parentheses: read the table once top-to-bottom the first time you use
this page, and after that the formulas should parse on sight.

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $S_t$ | kVCM / USD spot price at time $t$ | USD / kVCM |
| $P$ | protocol price (constant) | kVCM / tonne |
| $\pi_t := P \cdot S_t$ | USD protocol price per tonne | USD / tonne |
| $\lambda$ | deterministic retirement flow | tonnes / year |
| $T$ | horizon | years |
| $N := \lambda \cdot T$ | total tonnes retired over $[0,T]$ | tonnes |
| $f$ | fee rate | dimensionless |
| $Q$ | fixed USD quote per tonne (principal model) | USD / tonne |
| $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | pre-purchase (hedge) fraction | dimensionless |
| $I_T := \int_0^T S_t\,dt$ | time-integral of GBM, the shared random kernel | (USD/kVCM)·year |
| $R_{\mathrm{fee}} = f \cdot P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$ | fee-based revenue | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} = Q \cdot N - P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$ | back-to-back ($\alpha=0$) P&L | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{matched}} = N \cdot (Q - P \cdot S_0)$ | matched pre-purchase ($\alpha=1$), deterministic | USD |
| $\Pi_\alpha = (1-\alpha) \cdot \Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} + \alpha \cdot \Pi_{\mathrm{matched}}$ | partial pre-purchase ($\alpha \in [0,1]$) | USD |
| $Q^* = (1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T}-1)/(\mu T)$ | break-even quote equating $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}]$ | USD / tonne |

Quick reading guide for the less-obvious symbols:

- $P$ is a **protocol constant**: how many tokens the protocol wants per
  tonne of CO₂ retired. It doesn't move. The *dollar* price per tonne
  $\pi_t = P \cdot S_t$ moves only because the token price $S_t$ moves.
- $I_T$ is the **area under the price curve over the horizon** — if you
  sketched $S_t$ against time for $t \in [0,T]$, $I_T$ is the area under
  that sketch. It shows up repeatedly because integrating the price over
  a period of time is exactly what "pay the running market rate for a
  steady flow of tonnes" costs.
- $R_{\mathrm{fee}}$, $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$, $\Pi_{\mathrm{matched}}$, $\Pi_\alpha$ are the four different P&L
  variables compared in the §4 table below. $R_{\mathrm{fee}}$ is revenue (always
  $\geq 0$); the $\Pi$'s are profit-and-loss (can be negative).
- $Q^*$ is the "fair" per-tonne quote for the principal model — the price
  at which its expected P&L matches the fee model's. The last section of
  this report plots how $Q^*$ depends on $\mu$ and $T$.

## Single-run outputs

This section looks at **one** set of parameters — one drift, one
volatility, one horizon, one hedge ratio — and shows the full P&L
distribution the simulator produces for each book. Think of it as the
"zoomed-in" view. The later parameter-sweep section is the "zoomed-out"
view across many parameter combinations.

Loaded from `data/run-42.json` — produced by `npm run simulate -- --seed 42`.


```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = FileAttachment("data/run-42.json").json()
```

### Run parameters

The scorecard below echoes the parameters used for this run. Each tile is
a single number the simulator took as an input (or, in the case of $Q^*$,
computed directly from the other inputs). Some worth flagging:

- **Monte Carlo paths** is how many random trajectories were simulated.
  More paths = tighter statistics on the tails, at a linear cost in
  runtime.
- **$\mu, \sigma, T$** define the price process: an expected growth rate, an
  annualised volatility, and a horizon in years.
- **$\lambda$** is the steady retirement flow in tonnes/year — the "throughput"
  of the business.
- **$f, Q, \alpha$** are the three business levers: the fee markup, the fixed
  principal quote, and the hedge ratio.
- **$Q^*$** is the closed-form break-even quote — if the operator picks
  $Q = Q^*$, the fee and back-to-back principal books will have **equal
  expected P&L**, but as §5 below shows, their *distributions* are still
  very different. Matching means does not match risk.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Monte Carlo paths</div><div class="value">${run.params.nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Time steps / path</div><div class="value">${run.params.nSteps}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">μ (drift, /yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.mu}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (vol, /√yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.sigma}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (horizon, yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.T}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">λ (tonnes/yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.lambda.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">f (fee rate)</div><div class="value">${run.params.f}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q (USD/tonne)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.params.Q}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">α (hedge ratio)</div><div class="value">${run.params.alpha}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q* (break-even)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.closed.QStar.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### §4 — P&L moments: closed-form vs Monte Carlo

This is the central scorecard. Each row is one of the four books from
the research note (the fee book, and three principal variants: fully
matched `3a`, fully back-to-back `3b`, and partially hedged `3c`), and
each column is a summary statistic of that book's P&L distribution.

The table has two logical halves:

1. **Sanity check (first block of columns).** For quantities the Phase A
   note derives in closed form — expected P&L and its standard deviation
   — we print **both** the closed-form value and the Monte Carlo
   estimate. If our simulator is correct, these two should agree. The
   $z$ column formalises that: it is the MC mean's distance from the
   closed form, measured in standard errors of the MC mean. Rule of
   thumb: $|z| \leq 2$ looks healthy, $|z| > 3$ is worth investigating.
2. **Risk metrics (second block).** Quantities that can't be computed
   from the first two moments alone: VaR, CVaR, loss probability, and a
   Sharpe-like ratio. These come straight from the empirical
   distribution of simulated P&Ls.

All P&L numbers are in **USD over the horizon $T$** (not annualised, not
per-tonne).

<div class="legend-table">

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (cf) / (mc) | expected P&L — closed form vs Monte Carlo |
| $\pm \mathrm{CI}_{95}$ | ±95% half-width of the MC mean |
| SD (cf) / (mc) | standard deviation of P&L |
| $z$ | $(\text{MC} - \text{cf}) / \text{stderr}$; MC sanity check |
| $\mathrm{VaR}_{95} / \mathrm{VaR}_{99}$ | loss threshold exceeded with 5% / 1% probability |
| $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ | expected loss in the worst 5% / 1% tail |
| $\mathbb{P}[\Pi<0]$ | probability of a losing realisation |
| Sharpe | $\mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$, invariant under $f$-scaling for fee model |

</div>

**Risk metrics in plain English.**

- **$\mathrm{VaR}_{95}$ (Value-at-Risk at 95%).** A loss number. "With 5% probability
  we lose at least $\mathrm{VaR}_{95}$." It's the 5th-percentile of P&L, sign-flipped.
  A common executive-summary number, but it deliberately does **not**
  tell you how bad the worst 5% are — only the threshold at which that
  5% begins.
- **$\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ (Conditional VaR, also "Expected Shortfall").** The average
  loss *inside* that worst 5%. If $\mathrm{VaR}_{95}$ is the door to the tail, $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$
  is the average depth of the room beyond. CVaR is a strictly more
  informative risk measure than VaR when the tail is fat or skewed.
- **$\mathrm{VaR}_{99} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$.** Same, but the worst 1% rather than the worst 5%.
  Use these for rarer but more severe events.
- **$\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$.** The probability the book simply loses money at the
  horizon. A blunt headline metric.
- **Sharpe-like $= \mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$.** Expected reward per unit of risk —
  higher is better. For the fee model this number is invariant in $f$
  (doubling the fee doubles both mean and SD), so it captures
  *structural* risk/reward, not the pricing dial.

**Reading tips.**

- Row `fee`: revenue only (never negative), so VaR/CVaR are negative
  numbers, i.e. "worst case is still a gain, just a smaller one."
- Row `principal_3a`: by construction the matched book has no terminal
  randomness at all, so SD, CI95, and z are zero to machine precision,
  and VaR/CVaR simply echo the sign of the one deterministic P&L.
- Row `principal_3b`: the unhedged principal book. This is the row
  where the loss tail is real — compare its $\mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ against the others
  to see how much a bad price path costs.
- Row `principal_3c`: the partially-hedged book. Expect its SD and
  CVaR to be smaller than `3b`'s by roughly $(1-\alpha)^2$ — the variance
  formula from Phase A §3c.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.rows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

### §6 — Compensated Merton: closed-form means survive jumps

Phase C adds an optional Merton jump-diffusion overlay to the spot
process (see [Phase C](phase-c.qmd) for the live sliders, and the
[Phase A note](phase-a.qmd) §6 for the derivation). The key claim:
the drift compensation term $-\lambda_J \kappa$ with
$\kappa = e^{\mu_J + \sigma_J^2/2} - 1$ makes $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0
e^{\mu t}$ exact for **every** $(\lambda_J, \mu_J, \sigma_J)$, so the
Dufresne first moment $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$, the GBM closed-form means of
every book, and the break-even quote $Q^*$ all carry over unchanged.
Only variances and tail metrics are jump-sensitive.

The table below is the Phase B numerical verification of that claim.
It re-runs the main simulator under a canonical overlay
($\lambda_J = 3$/yr, $\mu_J = -0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$) chosen to be
fat enough that a variance-dependent formula would visibly
disagree — and compares the Merton Monte Carlo means against the
**GBM** closed-form means. The expected signature:

- $|z| \lesssim 2$ across all rows (means agree within MC noise),
- **SD (merton)** strictly greater than **SD (gbm-cf)** on every row
  (jumps inject extra variance the GBM formula cannot see).

If a row's $|z|$ is consistently large, the compensation wiring is
broken. If the Merton SD ever drops below the GBM-cf SD by more than
MC noise, the jump overlay is not firing.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.jumpCheck.rows, {
  columns: ["name","gbmClosedMean","mertonMcMean","mertonMcCi95","gbmClosedSd","mertonMcSd","zVsGbmClosed"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    gbmClosedMean: "E[Π] gbm-cf",
    mertonMcMean: "E[Π] merton-mc",
    mertonMcCi95: "±CI95",
    gbmClosedSd: "SD gbm-cf",
    mertonMcSd: "SD merton-mc",
    zVsGbmClosed: "z vs gbm-cf"
  },
  format: {
    gbmClosedMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    gbmClosedSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mertonMcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zVsGbmClosed: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
  }
})
```

_Overlay used: $\lambda_J = 3$/yr, $\mu_J = -0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$
(set by the CLI's `JUMP_CHECK` constant). Matching means is the
numerical signature of the compensation identity
$\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu t}$; widened SDs are the signature that
jumps are actually firing._

Spot-check on the same run:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] gbm closed</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.gbmClosed.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] merton mc</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.mertonMcMean.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">z vs gbm closed</div><div class="value">${(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed).toFixed(2)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### P&L distributions

The table above summarises each book with a handful of statistics. This
plot shows the whole distribution behind those statistics — one panel
per stochastic book. The shapes tell the same story the numbers told,
but visually.

How to read a panel:

- The **horizontal axis** is the book's terminal P&L in USD over the
  horizon $T$. Negative values are losses.
- The **bars** are a histogram of simulated P&L outcomes, rescaled as
  an empirical density.
- The **solid black vertical rule** sits at the closed-form expected
  P&L $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. If Monte Carlo is converged, the visual centre of the
  histogram should land right on it.
- The **dashed grey vertical rule** sits at $\Pi = 0$ — the break-even
  line. Whatever mass of the histogram sits to the left of it is the
  loss probability $\mathbb{P}[\Pi<0]$ reported in the table.

What to notice on each panel:

- **Fee book** ($R_{\mathrm{fee}} = f \cdot P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$). Pure revenue: the formula has no
  minus sign, so this random variable is non-negative by construction.
  The histogram sits entirely in the right half-plane (no mass to the
  left of $\Pi = 0$). Its shape is right-skewed — a long right tail —
  because it inherits the right-skew of $I_T$ (see next section).
- **Principal 3b** (back-to-back, $\alpha = 0$). Same random kernel $I_T$, but
  it enters with a **minus** sign, so the distribution is the mirror of
  the fee book's: upside is capped (best case: token price collapses to
  zero, company gets the full fixed payment $Q \cdot N$), downside is
  unbounded (worst case: token rallies, company has to buy expensively
  to fill the contract). This left-skewed loss tail is the central
  risk concern of the whole research project.
- **Principal 3c** (partial, $\alpha = 0.5$ in this run). Same qualitative
  shape as 3b, but narrower by a factor of $(1-\alpha)^2 = 0.25$ on variance,
  i.e. $(1-\alpha) = 0.5$ on standard deviation. This is the visual
  demonstration that pre-buying tokens shrinks the loss tail.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pnlData = [
  ...run.sampleTraces.fee.map(v => ({model: "fee", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.b2b.map(v => ({model: "principal_3b", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.partial.map(v => ({model: "principal_3c", pnl: v}))
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 420,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginRight: 30,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "terminal P&L Π  (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  fy: { label: "model" },
  color: { legend: true, domain: ["fee","principal_3b","principal_3c"], label: "model" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.rectY(pnlData, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, { x: "pnl", fill: "model", fy: "model", thresholds: 60 })),
    Plot.ruleX(run.rows.filter(r => ["fee","principal_3b","principal_3c"].includes(r.name)),
      { x: "closedFormMean", fy: "name", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black rule on each panel: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. Dashed grey rule:
break-even $\Pi = 0$._

### $I_T$ histogram vs. Dufresne closed-form moments

$I_T := \int_0^T S_t\,dt$ is the time-integral of the simulated price: the
area under the price-vs-time curve over the horizon. Why does this one
random number deserve its own plot?

Because every book's P&L turns out to be a simple linear function of
$I_T$. The fee book's revenue is $f \cdot P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$. The back-to-back
principal book's P&L is $Q \cdot N - P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$. The partially-hedged book is
an affine combination of those two. So $I_T$ is the **one random
variable** that drives everything stochastic in this notebook; every
other distribution is just a rescaling or reflection of this one.

Dufresne (2001) gives closed-form formulas for the mean and variance of
$I_T$, but not for its full distribution — $I_T$ is **not**
log-normally distributed, even though $S_t$ is, so tail quantiles have
to be estimated by Monte Carlo. This plot is that estimate:

- **Histogram bars** are the simulated density of $I_T$ across all
  paths.
- **Solid black rule** is the Dufresne closed-form mean
  $\mathbb{E}[I_T] = S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T}-1)/\mu$. If the simulator is correct, it should
  land on the visual centre of the histogram.
- **Dashed black rules** are $\mathbb{E}[I_T] \pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$ — roughly a one-sigma
  band. A well-behaved histogram puts ~68% of its mass between them.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
itHist = {
  const { edges, counts } = run.itSampleHistogram;
  const total = counts.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  return counts.map((c,i) => ({
    x0: edges[i], x1: edges[i+1], density: c / (total * (edges[i+1]-edges[i]))
  }));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "I_T = ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt   ((USD/kVCM)·year)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(itHist, { x1: "x0", x2: "x1", y: "density", fill: "#4c78a8", fillOpacity: 0.7 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean - Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance),
                run.closed.IT.mean + Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance)],
               { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3" })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$ (Dufresne closed form). Dashed black: $\mathbb{E}[I_T] \pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$._

### Sample GBM paths — matched-book NAV view (§3a)

The matched pre-purchase book ($\alpha = 1$) has a **deterministic** P&L at
the horizon — the operator locked in a sale price $Q$ per tonne and
also locked in a purchase cost $P \cdot S_0$ per tonne at day zero, so the
final number doesn't depend on the random path $S_t$ at all.

So why worry? Because in the *meantime* the business is carrying the
token inventory on its balance sheet, and that inventory's mark-to-
market value moves with the token price. If the token price tanks in
month three, the accounting value of the remaining tokens falls — and
that can trigger margin calls, covenant breaches, or simply painful
quarterly reporting, even if by the horizon the book is mathematically
fine.

The Phase A note defines this mark-to-market inventory value as

$$
V_t = (N \cdot P) \cdot (1 - t/T) \cdot S_t.
$$

Read the formula in parts:

- $N \cdot P$ is the total number of tokens originally bought.
- $(1 - t/T)$ is the fraction **still** in inventory at time $t$ — it
  starts at 1 (all of them) and decays linearly to 0 at the horizon,
  because retirements burn them at the constant rate $\lambda$.
- $S_t$ is the current dollar price per token.

So $V_t = (\text{tokens still held}) \times (\text{current price per token})$ — ordinary
mark-to-market. At $t = 0$ it equals $V_0 = N \cdot P \cdot S_0$, the original
purchase cost. At $t = T$ it equals zero, because the last token has
been retired and there is no inventory left to value.

The plot shows a handful of sampled paths of $V_t$:

- **Grey lines** are random trajectories — each one is a possible
  future history of the NAV.
- **Orange line** is the first path, highlighted so one trajectory is
  easy to follow with the eye.
- Notice that **every path pins to zero at $t = T$** regardless of
  whether the token price went up or down. That is the burn schedule
  doing its work.
- The scary bit is the **dip in the middle**. Even a path that
  recovers to a reasonable terminal NAV can touch a painful low
  partway through. The next plot quantifies that.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = run.sampledPaths.flatMap((path, idx) =>
  path.map((s, k) => ({
    t: k / run.params.nSteps,
    S: s,
    V: run.params.lambda * run.params.T * run.params.P * (1 - k / run.params.nSteps) * s,
    id: idx
  }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "normalised time  t / T", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "V_t  =  N·P·(1−t/T)·S_t    (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords, { x: "t", y: "V", z: "id", stroke: "#888", strokeOpacity: 0.3 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "t", y: "V", stroke: "#c94", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Grey lines: sampled Monte Carlo NAV paths. Orange line: the first path,
highlighted for visibility. $V_T$ is pinned to zero by the burn schedule
regardless of $S_T$._

### NAV shortfall vs. schedule (§3a)

For the matched book, the relevant solvency question is: "If the token
price had stayed flat at $S_0$ the whole time, the NAV would have decayed
along a straight line from $N \cdot P \cdot S_0$ down to zero. How far *below* that
straight line did the actual NAV dip, in the worst case?"

Phrased as a formula, that is

$$
D := \max_{t \leq T} \left[ N \cdot P \cdot (1 - t/T) \cdot (S_0 - S_t) \right]_+
$$

where $[x]_+$ means $\max(x, 0)$ — we only count the part where the actual
NAV is **below** the no-price-move schedule, not above it (an upward
excursion is a paper gain, not a solvency risk).

This histogram is the empirical distribution of that maximum shortfall
$D$ across all simulated paths:

- **The x-axis** is dollars of shortfall relative to the flat-price
  schedule. Further right = deeper dip = worse.
- **The dashed black rule** is the 95th percentile — a number that, on
  average, will be exceeded on 1 out of every 20 years simulated.
- **The solid black rule** is the 99th percentile — exceeded on 1 out
  of every 100. These two percentiles are the natural stress thresholds
  the treasury team would want to size its capital buffer against.

Even though the matched book has deterministic **terminal** P&L, this
plot shows why it is not "risk-free" in any practical sense: a large
interim drawdown is a real operational event.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "max_t [N·P·(1−t/T)·(S_0−S_t)]₊   (USD shortfall)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "number of paths", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(run.sampleTraces.navDrawdown, Plot.binX({y:"count"}, {x: d => d, fill:"#e45", fillOpacity: 0.8})),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var95], { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var99], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Dashed black: 95th percentile of the shortfall distribution. Solid black:
99th percentile. These are the natural stress thresholds for the matched
book even though its terminal P&L is deterministic._

## Parameter sweep

The single-run view above fixes all parameters and looks at the
distribution. This section does the opposite: it **fixes the book**
(principal 3c, the partially-hedged one) and a single risk summary
($\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$), and then sweeps the business parameters $(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ to see
how that risk summary responds.

Loaded from `data/sweep.json` — a grid of Monte Carlo runs over
$(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$. Use the controls below to slice the grid. The plots below
track $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ for the **principal 3c** (partial pre-purchase) book.
Reminder: **$\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ is the expected loss in the worst 5% of outcomes,
so larger numbers are worse.** A reader can think of the two plots
below as "how bad does the worst-5% outcome get, as I dial each knob?"

All P&L sweep quantities are in USD over horizon $T$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sweep = FileAttachment("data/sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickAlpha = Inputs.range([0,1], { step: 0.25, value: 0.5, label: "hedge ratio α" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMu = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.mus, { value: 0.05, label: "drift μ (/yr)" })
```

### Tail risk vs volatility $\sigma$ (fixed $\alpha, \mu$)

Holding $\alpha$ (the hedge ratio) and $\mu$ (the drift) fixed at the values
chosen above, this line shows how the $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ of the 3c book grows as
we crank up $\sigma$ (volatility).

**Intuition.** Volatility is the size of the random jitter in the token
price. More jitter means the integral $I_T$ is more spread out, which
means the principal book's P&L is more spread out, which means its
left tail is fatter — so the average loss inside that tail is bigger.
We therefore expect the curve below to be monotonically increasing
(higher volatility → worse tail loss). That's a useful sanity check:
if the line is non-monotone or flat, something is wrong upstream.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredBySigma = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.alpha === pickAlpha && c.mu === pickMu)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "volatility σ   (annualised)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "linear" },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (principal 3c)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#c53", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#c53", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

_Sensitivity of the 3c tail to $\sigma$ at the selected $(\alpha, \mu)$. A monotone rise
is the expected signature — vol directly inflates $\mathrm{Var}[I_T]$ and hence the
tail of $\Pi_\alpha$._

### Tail risk vs hedge ratio $\alpha$ (fixed $\mu$, $\sigma$ at grid[1])

This is the plot that answers the business question "how much do I
reduce my loss tail by pre-buying more tokens up front?"

It fixes $\mu$ at the selected value and $\sigma$ at the **second** value in
the grid (a middle-of-the-road volatility so the shape is easy to
read), then sweeps $\alpha$ from 0 (fully back-to-back, no pre-buying) to 1
(fully matched, entire year's supply bought today).

Phase A §3c showed that $\mathrm{Var}[\Pi_\alpha] = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \mathrm{Var}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$. Since CVaR
scales roughly with standard deviation in a broad family of
distributions, the $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ curve should collapse **quadratically**
toward zero as $\alpha \to 1$. Visually: expect a smooth convex curve that
bends down steeply, not a straight line. That's the signature of the
$(1-\alpha)^2$ factor.

At $\alpha = 1$ the book is fully pre-hedged and terminal P&L is
deterministic; at that point the $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ drops to the sign of the one
deterministic P&L (a single value, not a tail).

This plot is the visual answer to: *"Pre-buying tokens is the natural
hedge for the back-to-back principal book."* The curve below is the
dose-response curve for that hedge.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
byAlpha = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === sweep.grid.sigmas[1])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Hedge ratio α vs tail risk. Fixed μ (selected above), σ = second grid value. At α = 1 the book is deterministic so CVaR95 collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.",
  x: { label: "hedge ratio α   (0 = back-to-back, 1 = fully matched)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (principal 3c)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#35a", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

## Break-even quote surface $Q^*(\mu, T)$

This section answers the pricing question: "If I were going to switch
from the fee model to the principal model, what per-tonne quote $Q$
would I need to charge so that my **expected** revenue is the same?"
The answer is $Q^*$, and it depends on how the operator expects the
token price to drift ($\mu$) and how long the contract runs ($T$).

$Q^*$ is the USD quote that equalises the *expected* P&L of the
back-to-back principal book with the fee-based book:

$$
Q^* = (1 + f) \cdot P \cdot S_0 \cdot \frac{e^{\mu T} - 1}{\mu T}.
$$

To make the picture easier to read, we divide both sides by
$(1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0$ — this removes the uninteresting scaling (which is just
today's fee-quote per tonne) and leaves behind the interesting shape:

$$
\frac{Q^*}{(1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0} = \frac{e^{\mu T} - 1}{\mu T}.
$$

The ratio on the right is **dimensionless** — it is the factor by
which the principal quote has to exceed the fee quote to compensate
for expected price drift. A value of 1.0 means "quote the same as the
fee model"; a value above 1.0 means "charge a premium to cover
expected token appreciation"; a value below 1.0 means "can afford to
undercut the fee model because the token is expected to cheapen."

On the plot:

- Each coloured line is one value of drift $\mu$ from the grid (see
  legend). Colour goes from low-to-high drift via the viridis ramp.
- The **dashed horizontal rule at 1.0** is the $\mu = 0$ limit: if the
  token price is expected to stay flat, the principal operator should
  quote the fee quote $(1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0$ and the ratio is exactly 1.
- Lines for $\mu > 0$ sit **above** the dashed line (principal model
  must charge more to cover expected appreciation).
- Lines for $\mu < 0$ sit **below** the dashed line (principal model
  can charge less because it expects the token to cheapen, saving on
  inventory costs).
- The **longer the horizon $T$**, the more compound growth piles up,
  and the further the line moves away from 1. Short horizons barely
  depart from the dashed line at all.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstar = FileAttachment("data/qstar-surface.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstarRecords = qstar.mus.flatMap((mu, i) =>
  qstar.Ts.map((T, j) => ({ mu, T, Q: qstar.values[i][j] }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 120,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "horizon T   (years)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "Q*  /  ((1+f)·P·S₀)     (dimensionless)", labelAnchor: "center" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "drift μ (/yr)", scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      stroke: "mu",
      strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      fill: "mu",
      r: 3
    })
  ]
})
```

_Curves for each drift $\mu$ in the grid. Positive drift lifts $Q^*$ above the
fee-based quote (principal must charge more to compensate for expected
kVCM appreciation); negative drift pushes it below. The $\mu = 0$ curve
collapses onto the dashed unit line._

## Reading notes

A few things to keep in mind while skimming or coming back to this
report later.

**On the numbers you see:**

- All closed-form / Monte Carlo agreement checks live in the $z$ column
  of the §4 table — a single glance should tell you whether the MC is
  well-converged at the current `nPaths`. Consistently large $|z|$
  values are the first thing to investigate; they almost always point
  to either a bug in the simulator or too few paths for the statistic
  you're reading.
- The fee and back-to-back books share the *same* random kernel $I_T$,
  so their variance ratio is the exact algebraic multiple $(P/f)^2$. If
  you ever read a ratio that disagrees with that constant, you are
  looking at either a simulator bug or a distribution other than the
  one this report claims to be showing.
- All P&L numbers are **totals over the horizon $T$**, not annualised
  and not per-tonne. If you want a per-tonne number, divide by
  $N = \lambda \cdot T$.
- "Loss" throughout means "the P&L random variable $\Pi$ came out
  negative." The fee book's P&L variable $R_{\mathrm{fee}}$ is really a revenue,
  so it is never negative — its VaR/CVaR as printed are the *low end*
  of revenue, not a loss against zero.

For cross-phase business take-aways, scope caveats, and a drawable
price-curve stress-test tool, see the [Conclusions](conclusions.qmd)
page.
